steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks for the function of the kestrel reference in the poem's overall argument. The friend describes inner calm brought by "the lack of anvil and strife." The speaker then says the wooing kestrel "mutes his mating-note to please the harmony of this sweet silence." The kestrel is doing something — suppressing its call — in response to the same silence. The explanation must account for what that action contributes to the poem's meaning.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'It emphasizes the speaker\'s belief that her friend is not sharing his true feelings with her.': The poem says the two understood each other perfectly without words: "each knew all the other said." There is no signal of withheld feelings or distrust. ✗
  • Choice B — 'It suggests that nature has secrets that humans are fundamentally incapable of discovering.': The kestrel is described in plain, observable terms — it mutes its call. No hidden mystery or human inability is implied. ✗
  • Choice C — 'It illustrates the assertion that peace is a subjective state rather than an objective one.': The poem never frames the silence as subjective. Both the speaker and the friend experience the same calm, and the kestrel responds to the same external silence — suggesting it is a shared, objective condition. ✗
  • Choice D — 'It presents the natural world as aligning with the experience of the speaker and her friend.': The kestrel modifies its behavior — muting its mating call — to preserve the same sweet silence the friend described as calming his soul. Nature mirrors the human experience. This is exactly what the kestrel example contributes. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
